import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

In [9]:
data["duration"]=data["duration"].fillna(0)
data["reason"]=data["reason"].fillna(0)
In [10]:
type(data['date_time_in'].iloc[0])
Out[10]:
str

"date_time_in" is a string, we have to convert it to datetime.

In [11]:
data["date_time_in"] = pd.to_datetime(data["date_time_in"], errors="coerce")

  data["date_time_in"] = pd.to_datetime(data["date_time_in"], errors="coerce")

data['final_grade'].unique()
Out[14]:
array(['B', 'A', 'F', 'C', 'D', 'W6', 'W', 'RC', 'QF', 'WH', 'RF', 'RQF',

In [15]:
custom_map = {'W6':'W','WH':'W','RWH':'W','RF':'F','RQF':'F','QF':'F','RA':'A','RC':'C','RD':'D','RB':'B','RP':'C','P':'C'}

data['custom_final_grade'] = data['final_grade'].replace(custom_map)
In [16]:
custom_final_grade_new={'A':4,'B':3,'C':2,'D':1,'F':0,'W':0}
data['final_grade_new']=data['custom_final_grade'].replace(custom_final_grade_new)

C:\Users\Owner\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_13020\19137244.py:2: FutureWarning: Downcasting behavior in `replace` is deprecated and will be removed in a future version. To retain the old behavior, explicitly call `result.infer_objects(copy=False)`. To opt-in to the future behavior, set `pd.set_option('future.no_silent_downcasting', True)`
  data['final_grade_new']=data['custom_final_grade'].replace(custom_final_grade_new)

In [30]:
student_summary = data.groupby('encrypt_id').agg(
    total_visits=('encrypt_id', 'count'),
    total_duration=('duration', 'sum'),
    grade_num=('final_grade_new', 'max')
).reset_index()

Center Summaries¶

In [34]:
center_summary = data.groupby('center').agg(
    total_visits=('center', 'count'),
    avg_grade=('final_grade_new', 'mean')
).reset_index()
In [35]:
center_summary
Out[35]:
center total_visits avg_grade
0 LIBERAL ARTS 13034 2.849087
1 MATH AND BUSINESS 25143 2.348884
2 SCIENCE 41661 2.824704
3 WORKSHOPS 199 3.301508
4 WRITING 11863 2.376802
In [36]:
plt.figure()

plt.scatter(center_summary['total_visits'], center_summary['avg_grade'])

for i, txt in enumerate(center_summary['center']):
    plt.annotate(txt, (center_summary['total_visits'][i], center_summary['avg_grade'][i]))

plt.xlabel("Total Visits")
plt.ylabel("Average Grade")
plt.title("Center Usage vs Average Grade")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interesting! Maybe we can confirm by computing the correlation

In [37]:
corr = center_summary['total_visits'].corr(center_summary['avg_grade'])
print("Correlation:", corr)

Correlation: -0.36757378191531415

Biology Total Duration - Grade Analysis¶

In [38]:
bio_data = data[data['course'].str.startswith('BIOL')]
In [39]:
bio_student = bio_data.groupby('encrypt_id').agg(
    total_visits=('encrypt_id', 'count'),
    total_duration=('duration', 'sum'),
    grade=('final_grade_new', 'first')
).reset_index()
In [40]:
bio_student.groupby('grade')[['total_visits', 'total_duration']].mean()
Out[40]:
total_visits total_duration
grade
0 5.773438 6.812161
1 7.093750 7.876875
2 7.749271 8.779329
3 8.774545 9.196491
4 10.808989 10.863438
In [41]:
plt.figure()
bio_student.boxplot(column='total_duration', by='grade')

plt.xlabel("Grade")
plt.ylabel("Total Duration")
plt.title("Biology Attendance by Grade")

plt.suptitle("")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

<Figure size 640x480 with 0 Axes>
In [42]:
corr = bio_student['total_duration'].corr(bio_student['grade'])

print("Correlation:", f"{corr:.4g}")

Correlation: 0.1208
In [43]:
corr_visits = bio_student['total_visits'].corr(bio_student['grade'])
corr_duration = bio_student['total_duration'].corr(bio_student['grade'])

print("Visits correlation:", f"{corr_visits:.4g}")
print("Duration correlation:", f"{corr_duration:.4g}")

Visits correlation: 0.1789
Duration correlation: 0.1208

What I see here??¶

Analysis of Biology courses shows that students with higher attendance tend to achieve slightly better grades, although the relationship is not perfectly linear (due to a very weak correlation), indicating that additional factors beyond attendance influence performance.

This likely means:

  1. Some students who struggle attend more (pulling correlation down)
  2. Some high-performing students don’t need many visits
  3. Attendance is not the only factor affecting grades

Peak Time Analysis¶

1. Monthly attendance (by academic year)¶
In [44]:
##Extracting year and month
data['year'] = data['date_time_in'].dt.year
data['month'] = data['date_time_in'].dt.month
data['month_name'] = data['date_time_in'].dt.month_name()
In [45]:
# Montly attendance

monthly_counts = data.groupby(['term', 'month_name']).size().reset_index(name='visits')
In [46]:
monthly_counts.head()
Out[46]:
term month_name visits
0 202310 August 478
1 202310 November 2132
2 202310 October 4111
3 202310 September 4555
4 202320 April 2300
In [47]:
data['term'].unique()
Out[47]:
array([202310, 202320, 202410, 202420, 202510, 202520, 202610])
In [48]:
for t in data['term'].unique():
    
    subset = monthly_counts[monthly_counts['term'] == t]

    # order months correctly
    month_order = ['January','February','March','April','May','June',
                   'July','August','September','October','November','December']

    subset['month_name'] = pd.Categorical(subset['month_name'], categories=month_order, ordered=True)
    subset = subset.sort_values('month_name')

    plt.figure()
    plt.plot(subset['month_name'], subset['visits'], marker='o')

    plt.xlabel("Month")
    plt.ylabel("Number of Visits")
    plt.title(f"Monthly Attendance Pattern (Term {t})")

    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

C:\Users\Owner\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_13020\3748936913.py:9: SettingWithCopyWarning:

Try using .loc[row_indexer,col_indexer] = value instead

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy
  subset['month_name'] = pd.Categorical(subset['month_name'], categories=month_order, ordered=True)
In [ ]:
2. Week of month analysis¶
In [49]:
data['day'] = data['date_time_in'].dt.day
data['week_of_month'] = (data['day'] -1) // 7 +1
In [50]:
data['week_of_month'].head(10)

In [51]:
week_counts = data.groupby(['month_name', 'week_of_month']).size().reset_index(name='visits')
month_selected = week_counts['month_name']
In [52]:
week_counts.sample(9)
Out[52]:
month_name week_of_month visits
3 April 4 2395
1 April 2 2273
36 September 4 5135

In [53]:
data['date'] = data['date_time_in'].dt.date
In [54]:
daily_counts = data.groupby(['month_name', 'week_of_month', 'date']).size().reset_index(name='daily_visits')
In [55]:
week_avg = daily_counts.groupby(['month_name', 'week_of_month'])['daily_visits'].mean().reset_index()
In [56]:
for m in week_avg['month_name'].unique():
    subset = week_avg[week_avg['month_name'] == m]

    plt.figure()
    plt.bar(subset['week_of_month'], subset['daily_visits'])

    plt.xlabel("Week of Month")
    plt.ylabel("Average Daily Visits")
    plt.title(f"Average Daily Visits per Week in {m}")

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
In [ ]:
